coredata.dc:title: A dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric sensor for the detection of spermine and spermidine in food based on N-doped carbon dots and peroxidase-like activity of V6O13 nanobelts
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Andi
Xia, Lian
Chen, Jiali
Wu, Siyu
Tang, Yue
Wu, Yuangen
coredata.dc:description: Spermine (SPM) and spermidine (SPD) exist widely in food, and their excessive consumption will cause serious harm to human body, so it is significant that determine the content of them in foods. Herein, the peroxidase-like activity of V6O13 nanobelts could catalyze n-ethyl-N-(3-sulfopropyl)-3-methylaniline sodium salt (TOPS) and 4-amino-antipyrine (4-AAP) to generate the purple complex (PC1). It was first found that PC1 could quench the CDs fluorescence by static quenching (SQE). The SPM and SPD can inhibit the peroxidase-like activity of V6O13, making changes in the color and fluorescence intensity of the solution. Therefore, a fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode sensor were constructed. The quantitative analysis of SPM and SPD will be realized through the variation of dual signal. Such sensor offers good detection sensitivity for SPM and SPD. The limit of detection (LOD) of SPM and SPD on colorimetric channel were 3.42 μg·L−1 and 11.10 μg·L−1, separately. In the fluorescent channels, the LOD of SPM and SPD were individually determined as low as 1.16 μg·L−1 and 1.31 μg·L−1. Particularly, the results of the dual-channel could be mutually validated, which improved the reliability of the assay. Such method demonstrated good detection of SPM and SPD in actual samples, indicating its huge potential for practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanozyme
Biogenic amine
Static quenching
Visual analysis
Dual signals

Dual-mode detection of SPM and SPD
Effect of V6O13 nanobelts on CDs
Principle of sensors for SPM and SPD
Feasibility of sensors for SPM and SPD
Inhibition mechanism of SPM and SPD
Selectivity and anti-interference of dual-signal sensors
Analysis of SPM and SPD in actual samples
Stability and reusability of dual-signal sensors

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32360621, 32160603, 31760486), the Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program (ZK[2021]Key Project 037), the Guizhou Provincial Key Technology R&D Program (QKHZC[2023]123), the Guizhou Provincial Outstanding Young Science and Technology Talent Project ([2021]5628), and the General Project for Technological Innovation and Development in the Field of Social Development in Guiyang City.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Normalized excitation and emission spectra of CDs combined with absorption spectra of PC1. (B) Absorption spectra of PC1 (Sample 1) and PC1-CDs (Sample 2). (C) Stern-Volmer linear equations for F0/F and different contents of V6O13 at 297 K, 310 K, and 318 K. Error bars indicated standard deviations across three repetitive assays (n = 3). (D) Transient PL spectra of CDs and CDs + PC1.

(A) Absorption spectra and visual images in various solutions. Sample 1: CDs + V6O13 + H2O2 + TOPS + 4-AAP; Sample 2: 30 μg·L−1 SPM + Sample 1; Sample 3: 50 μg·L−1 SPM + Sample 1; Sample 4: 100 μg·L−1 SPM + Sample 1; Sample 5: CDs + H2O2 + TOPS + 4-AAP. (B) Fluorescence spectra in various solutions (Ex: 375 nm; Em: 432 nm). Sample 1: CDs; Sample 2: CDs + V6O13 + H2O2 + TOPS + 4-AAP; Sample 3: 30 μg·L−1 SPM + Sample 2; Sample 4: 50 μg·L−1 SPM + Sample 2; Sample 5: 100 μg·L−1 SPM + Sample 2; Sample 6: CDs + 100 μg·L−1 SPM. (cV6O13 = 4 μg·mL−1, cH2O2= 12 mM, c4-AAP = 0.2 mM, cTOPS = 0.2 mM, cNaAc = 20 mM, pH 5.0).

(A) The influence of p-benzoquinone on the relative activity of V6O13 in diverse solutions. Error bars indicated standard deviations across three repetitive assays (n = 3). (B) Fluorescence spectra of various systems (Ex: 315 nm; Em: 430 nm). Sample 1: V6O13 + H2O2 + terephthalic acid; Sample 2: 50 μg·L−1 SPM + Sample 1; Sample 3: 100 μg·L−1 SPM + Sample 1; Sample 4: H2O2 + terephthalic acid. (C) The peak current of the mixture (TOPS + 4-AAP) for various reaction solutions. Sample 1: TOPS + 4-AAP; Sample 2: H2O2 + TOPS + 4-AAP; Sample 3: V6O13 + Sample 2; Sample 4: 50 μg·L−1 SPM + Sample 3; Sample 5: 100 μg·L−1 SPM + Sample 3. (cV6O13 = 4 μg·mL−1, cH2O2= 12 mM, c4-AAP = 0.2 mM, cTOPS = 0.2 mM, cNaAc = 20 mM, pH 5.0).

(A) Sensitivity of the colorimetric signal for the SPM. Image of colorimetric solution and relation of absorbance change (ΔA) with SPM content. Inset: Absorbance change (ΔA) curve of reaction solution at low content of SPM (y = 0.0051x + 0.0451, R2 = 0.9904). (B) Sensitivity of fluorescent signals for the SPM. Image of fluorescent signal and relation of fluorescence change (ΔF) with SPM content. Inset: Fluorescence change (ΔF) curve of reaction solution at low quantities of SPM (y = 1.3283x + 63.4037, R2 = 0.9924). (C) Correlation of SPM detection results for fluorescent and colorimetric channels. Error bars in figures indicated standard deviations across three repetitive assays (n = 3).

(A) Selectivity of the constructed dual-signal sensor in colorimetric signals. (B) Selectivity of the constructed dual-signal sensor in fluorescent signals. Error bars in figures indicated standard deviations across three repetitive assays (n = 3).
Schematic illustration of a dual-signal sensing system for detecting SPMs and SPDs.

head.ce:title: A dual-mode colorimetric and fluorometric sensor for the detection of spermine and spermidine in food based on N-doped carbon dots and peroxidase-like activity of V6O13 nanobelts

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guizhou Province Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering and Biopharmacy, School of Liquor and Food Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guizhou Province Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering and Biopharmacy, School of Liquor and Food Engineering, Guizhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guizhou Province Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering and Biopharmacy, School of Liquor and Food Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Sciences, Guizhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Sciences, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: School of Liquor and Food Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Liquor and Food Engineering, Guizhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Spermine (SPM) and spermidine (SPD) exist widely in food, and their excessive consumption will cause serious harm to human body, so it is significant that determine the content of them in foods. Herein, the peroxidase-like activity of V6O13 nanobelts could catalyze n-ethyl-N-(3-sulfopropyl)-3-methylaniline sodium salt (TOPS) and 4-amino-antipyrine (4-AAP) to generate the purple complex (PC1). It was first found that PC1 could quench the CDs fluorescence by static quenching (SQE). The SPM and SPD can inhibit the peroxidase-like activity of V6O13, making changes in the color and fluorescence intensity of the solution. Therefore, a fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode sensor were constructed. The quantitative analysis of SPM and SPD will be realized through the variation of dual signal. Such sensor offers good detection sensitivity for SPM and SPD. The limit of detection (LOD) of SPM and SPD on colorimetric channel were 3.42 μg·L−1 and 11.10 μg·L−1, separately. In the fluorescent channels, the LOD of SPM and SPD were individually determined as low as 1.16 μg·L−1 and 1.31 μg·L−1. Particularly, the results of the dual-channel could be mutually validated, which improved the reliability of the assay. Such method demonstrated good detection of SPM and SPD in actual samples, indicating its huge potential for practical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Spermine (SPM) and spermidine (SPD) are aliphatic biogenic amines with a similar structure. The residues of SPM and SPD are widely found in foods such as pork, mushrooms, and corn, which can form the carcinogen nitrosamine by reacting with nitrite [1–3]. High levels of SPM or SPD can cause adverse reactions, such as headache, vomiting and palpitations [4]. Hence, it is crucial that a specific, accurate and highly sensitive method for detecting SPM and SPD in food is established. Capillary electrophoresis (CE) [5], gas chromatography (GC) [6] and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7] are common methods for detecting SPM and SPD. These methods offer benefits such as accurate results, high sensitivity, and broad applicability. Unfortunately, the aforementioned procedures necessitate complex pre-treatments, sophisticated instruments, and trained personnel to identify SPM or SPD quickly. Recently, colorimetric and fluorescent techniques for SPM and SPD detection have been reported [8,9]. However, these methods apply a single fluorescence or absorbance as the detection signal are easily affected by anthropic factors or the surrounding environment, resulting in inaccurate detection results.

Dual-signal sensors that combine colorimetric and fluorescent methods can largely eliminate instrumental and human errors, making the sensors better reliability. Recently, many researchers have developed sensing strategies with colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode for cell imaging [10], drug monitoring [11], and food detection [12,13]. So far, the existing colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode assays can fall into two main strategies. The most common strategy is that the aggregation discoloration effect of AuNPs is applied as the colorimetric signal, while the fluorescence energy resonance transfer (FRET) between AuNPs and fluorescent dyes is employed as the fluorescent signal [14,15]. However, AuNPs is easily destabilized by cationic polymers and high-salt particles, which forms irreversible aggregation and leads to inaccurate results. Furthermore, the sensors of FRET mechanism usually require complex probe modifications and depend on the spatial distance between the fluorescent donor and acceptor, resulting some limitations in their detection applications. Another common strategy is to apply the fluorescent signal, which originates from the internal filtering effect (IFE) between the MnO2 nanoparticles and CdTe, and the chromogenic changes produced by the simulated oxidase activity of nanoparticles as the colorimetric signal [16,17]. Besides, the MnO2 nanoparticles suffers from defects like low catalytic activity, poor stability and small surface area, which limit its application in trace analysis. Sensors based on the IFE mechanism are also susceptible to interference from environmental factors, causing low detection sensitivity and unstable results. Moreover, the current fluorescent materials such as rhodamine B, organic fluorophores and nanoclusters have intractable problems like poor photostability and complex synthesis. Currently, dual signal sensors for detecting SPM and SPD in food have not been reported. Therefore, to search for colorimetric and fluorescent sensing materials with good stability performance, which is of great significance for constructing stable and reliable dual signal SPM and SPD sensors in food.

V6O13 nanobelts is considered to be an outstanding material with many active sites at the edges and vast specific surface area [18]. In addition, due to its good peroxide mimic enzyme activity, different chromogenic substrates can be catalyzed by V6O13 nanobelts to produce color changes when H2O2 is present, which can be employed for the ideal carrier as colorimetric signals. Our group has applied to the peroxidase activity of V6O13 and establish a colorimetric sensor for detecting metal ions [19]. However, the employment of peroxidase-like activity of V6O13 has not yet been reported in dual-signal detection. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) is fluorescent nanoparticles with spherical form and a size of less than 10 nm, which have been widely applied in food preservation, biological imaging, drug delivery and tumor therapy [20–23]. The CDs has been employed to colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode assay in place of other fluorescent materials, due to its advantages in stability, cost, water solubility and biocompatibility [24,25]. Unfortunately, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms are mostly IFE and FRET in the dual-mode analysis of CDs. At present, the development of dual-mode strategies with other quenching mechanisms is still a challenge.

To overcome the drawbacks of the above approaches, a new strategy is proposed to construct a dual-mode rapid determination for SPM and SPD in foodstuffs using V6O13 and CDs as dual-signal materials. The strategy utilizes the color change produced by the V6O13 peroxidase activity catalyzing the chromogenic substrate as a colorimetric signal, while the CDs fluorescence is quenching by the catalytic chromogenic product as the fluorescent signal. The color and fluorescence intensity of the system are changed by the inhibitory effect of SPM and SPD on V6O13 nanobelts. The correspondence between the variations in dual signals and the content of SPM and SPD can realize convenient quantitative detection of SPM and SPD. The quenching mechanism of CDs by the catalytic product is discussed through fluorescence spectrum, UV absorption spectra, fluorescence lifetime and Stern-Volmer linear equation. This work provides an innovative approach for the development of dual-mode method with static quenching (SQE). Further, the strategy has the ascendancies of low expense, good stability and easy procedure, and avoids the complicated material preparation process. Such advantages make the established sensor promising for visual inspection and provide a novel approach for simultaneous detection of SPM and SPD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of V6O13 nanobelts and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The reagents, instruments, determination of free radicals, electrochemical experiment, and analysis of actual samples are shown in Supporting Information. Scheme S1 exhibited the preparation process of V6O13 nanobelts and CDs. The preparation of V6O13 nanobelts was referred to the previous reported literature [19]. Briefly, 18 mL of ultrapure water was mixed evenly with 0.2275 g of V2O5 powder using a magnetic stirrer. Then, 1 mL anhydrous ethanol and 1 mL H2O2 were dissolved sequentially to the solution mentioned above. It was transferred to a Teflon liner inside a sealed and heated at 250 ℃ for 48 h. The product was filtered out and washed three times with ultrapure water and anhydrous ethanol, respectively. Then, dried it in a vacuum at 75 ℃ for 12 h to acquire the powder.

The preparation of CDs was based on existing reported methods [26]. The 0.108 g o-phenylenediamine and 0.192 g citric acid were added to 10 mL ultrapure water and treated with ultrasound for 10 min. A solution was sealed in a Teflon liner and kept at 120 ℃ for 6 h, and cooled at 25 ℃. Then, the solution turned dark red, and the discolored product was dialysis (500 MW) for 12 h. Finally, a filter membrane (0.22 μm) was applied to filter the prepared solution, and the obtained CDs solution was stored at 4 ℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-mode detection of SPM and SPD
ce:section.ce:para: The 10 μL SPM or SPD solutions were combined with 10 μL V6O13 in a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube, and then incubated at 30 ℃ for 10 min. Then, a 500 μL system was formed by adding 460 μL NaAc buffer, 10 μL H2O2, 10 μL TOPS, 10 μL 4-AAP, and 10 μL CDs solution to the centrifuge tube. An equal volume of ultrapure water was applied for the control instead of SPM or SPD solution. The colorimetric signal was evaluated by the change of absorbance value (∆A=A0-A1) at 550 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence recovery value (∆FF-F0) at 432 nm was employed for the fluorescent signal.

The results in Supporting Information demonstrated the V6O13 nanobelts and CDs had been successfully prepared. The Fig. S1A -B was the SEM image and the XRD spectrum of V6O13 nanobelts. The elements and groups of V6O13 were analyzed by XPS (Fig. S1C). The results in the Fig. S1D indicated that enlarged XPS spectra of V6O13 nanobelts between 513 and 528 eV. The TEM images and their particle size distribution diagram of CDs were displayed in Fig. S2A -B. The results in the Fig. S2C illustrated the surface of CDs was investigated using XPS spectra. High-resolution XPS spectra of the C1s, N 1 s and O1s were showed in Fig. S2D -F. The XRD pattern of CDs was revealed by Fig. S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of V6O13 nanobelts on CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared V6O13 possessed excellent peroxidase activity and was able to catalyze TOPS and 4-AAP to generate the purple complex (PC1) under the action of H2O2 (Fig. S4). Further, it was found that the PC1 can quench CDs fluorescence (Fig. S5). The detailed description is displayed in Supporting Information. To examine the quenching mechanism of CDs by PC1, fluorescence emission spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption experiments were performed. The excitation and emission spectra of CDs were not effectively covered by the absorption spectra of PC1 ( Fig. 1 A). Compared with PC1 (Fig. 1 B), the absorbance of PC1-CDs decreased slightly at 550 nm. Moreover, the influence of temperature on the quenching constant was explored. Fig. 1 C shows that the linear slope, Ksv, diminishes as temperature rises, indicating that SQE has an impact on it [27]. Further, there did not exhibit discernible difference in the fluorescence lifetime of PC1-CDs compared to CDs (Fig. 1 D). It was displayed that PC1 quenched CDs fluorescence through SQE. The detailed description is displayed in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of sensors for SPM and SPD
ce:section.ce:para: The detection principle was illustrated in Scheme 1. In the presence of H2O2, PC1 was produced by V6O13 catalyzing TOPS and 4-AAP, which caused fluorescence quenching of CDs through SQE. The interaction of SPM or SPD with V6O13 could decrease the activity of V6O13, which reduced the production of PC1 and restored the CDs fluorescence. Once SPM or SPD was absented in the system, the solution exhibited a purple color and a low fluorescent signal. In contrast, the SPM or SPD was presented in the system, the solution appeared a lighter purple and a higher fluorescence. Moreover, the system displayed a positive correlation between the concentration of SPM or SPD and the change of color and fluorescence. Thus, the SPM and SPD dual-mode detection methods were constructed by the PC1 UV absorption and the CDs fluorescence as test signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of sensors for SPM and SPD
ce:section.ce:para: Absorption spectra and color changes of various reaction solutions within the wavelength range of 400 -700 nm were recorded with SPM as the detection target to explore the feasibility of the dual-signal sensors ( Fig. 2 A). The V6O13 could catalyze the oxidation of TOPS and 4-AAP to produce PC1 with H2O2, which turned the solution purple and produced an apparent absorption peak at 550 nm (Sample 1). Once the SPM was present (Samples 2–4), it can be observed that the color of the solution changed from purple to light purple as the content of SPM raised. The absorption peak at 550 nm also gradually became weaker. However, the mixture of CDs, H2O2, TOPS and 4-AAP had no effect on the sensor (Sample 5). The fluorescence changes of different solutions and their fluorescence emission spectra in 400–600 nm were noted (Fig. 2 B). The pure CDs revealed an apparent fluorescence peak at 432 nm and emitted a brilliant fluorescence when illuminated by 365 nm ultraviolet light (Sample 1). The CDs fluorescence was weakened with the formation of PC1, after adding V6O13, H2O2, TOPS, and 4-AAP (Sample 2). As the SPM content increased, the fluorescence emission peaks rose steadily, and the fluorescent signals of the solution progressively improved (Samples 3–5). Notably, the CDs fluorescence was unaffected by pure SPM (Sample 6). The above results revealed that SPM effectively inhibited the peroxidase activity of V6O13, leading to a decrease in the production of PC1 and a consequent restoration of CDs fluorescence. Consequently, both the production of PC1 and the CDs fluorescence could be employed the quantitative analysis of SPM. The SPD also had a similar inhibitory effect on V6O13 nanobelts as SPM (Fig. S6). Hence, it was possible to detect SPD and SPM using the constructed colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition mechanism of SPM and SPD
ce:section.ce:para: The p-benzoquinone was employed as inhibitor to track ·O2 - in the presence or absence of SPM [28]. The quantity of ·O2 - did not change with SPM ( Fig. 3 A). Such phenomena suggested that ·O2 - had no impact on the peroxidase activity of V6O13 nanobelts. Terephthalic acid was applied as a fluorescence tracer to monitor ·OH quantity [29]. As depicted in Fig. 3 B, V6O13 catalyzed the generation of ·OH from H2O2, and ·OH combined with terephthalic acid to generate a great quantity of fluorescent substances (Sample 1). An increasing SPM quantity caused a reduction in the system fluorescence (Samples 2–3). To further elucidate the mechanism by which SPM inhibits the peroxidase activity of V6O13, cyclic voltammetry was applied to measure current variations in various solutions. As revealed in Fig. 3 C, the mixture of 4-AAP and TOPS had a 4.05 μA peak current in the NaAc buffer (Sample 1). Once H2O2 and the mixture of V6O13 and H2O2 were contained in the reaction system, the peak currents of the mixtures changed from 4.05 μA to 5.01 μA (Sample 2) and 8.91 μA (Sample 3), respectively. The peak current of the mixtures decreased to 7.26 μA (Sample 4) or even 6.16 μA (Sample 5) with increasing SPM amount. These phenomena indicate that SPM can block the transfer of electrons from the V6O13 nanobelts to H2O2 and inhibit the production of ·OH, so that the peroxidase activity of V6O13 was reduced. Similarly, SPD also inhibited the production of ·OH and reduced the peroxidase activity of V6O13 (Fig. S7).

Some factors like buffer, enzyme concentration, substrate, reaction temperature, and reaction time, were explored separately to obtain the optimal detection results. The best results can be achieved under the following experimental circumstances: (a) The optimal buffer has a pH value of 5.0 with a concentration of 20 mM (Fig. S8); (b) a 4 μg·mL−1content of V6O13 nanobelts, a 600 mM amount of H2O2 and best concentrations of TOPS and 4-AAP are both 10 mM (Fig. S9); (c) optimal incubation temperature is 30 ℃ and the optimal reaction time is 20 min (Fig. S10).

It was evaluated the sensitivity of dual-signal sensors by varying the of SPM content. In the colorimetric channel, the absorbance at 550 nm decreased with the enhancement of SPM content, and a linear relationship was observed from 30 to 80 μg·L−1. The detection limit (LOD) was 3.42 μg·L−1( Fig. 4 A). In the fluorescent channel, the fluorescence enhanced progressively with the SPM content, and there was a strong linear relationship between 40 and 100 μg·L−1. The LOD was calculated as 1.16 μg·L−1(Fig. 4 B). Fig. 4 C shows the relationship between the amounts of SPM studied through fluorescent (y-axis) and colorimetric (x-axis) channels. The results showed that there was a strong linear correlation between fluorescent channel and colorimetric channel (R2=0.9923). Considering that the peroxidase activity of V6O13 was also inhibited by SPD, the sensor could also be applied to detect SPD (Fig. S11). The contents of SPD (40–200 μg·L−1) were all in good linear relationship with ΔA and ΔF with reference to the SPM treatment. The LOD of the colorimetric channel is 11.10 μg·L−1, while the fluorescent channel is 1.31 μg·L−1. Additionally, a strong linear correlation (R2=0.9917) was found between the fluorescence and colorimetric channels. Overall, it was illustrated that the sensor has good detection sensitivity for SPM and SPD. The above data expressed that there was a good linear relationship between the colorimetric channel and the fluorescent channel. It was indicated that the dual-mode detection data can be mutually checked, guaranteeing the dependability for SPM and SPD detection. Herein, a comparison between the sensor and other techniques such as electrochemistry, capillary electrophoresis combined with chemiluminescence, fluorescence and colorimetry has been carried out (Table S1). Encouragingly, the LOD of the present method is significantly lower and its results have better reliability and accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of dual-signal sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The absorbance values and the fluorescence responses of the system for different compounds were determined to explore the selectivity and anti-interference of the dual-signal sensors for SPM and SPD. As expressed in Fig. 5, the solution appeared almost colorless and emitted bright fluorescence only in presence of SPM and SPD. Moreover, the variation in absorbance and fluorescence intensity of the sensor almost same after the addition of interfering substances (Fig. S12). The structures of SPM and SPD were compared with other biogenic amines (Fig. S13). It is worth noting that only SPM and SPD contain imine group (-N-H-). It was speculated that the imine nitrogen atom of SPM and SPD could coordinate with the metal atom in V6O13, thereby blocking the electron transfer between V6O13 and H2O2 and reducing the production of ·OH [30]. Thus, the peroxidase activity of V6O13 was reduced. In contrast, other biogenic amines without such group could not inhibit the peroxidase activity of V6O13. In addition, tryptamine and histamine contained benzene ring or phenyl-like ring macromolecules might form a certain steric hindrance, which prevented them from interacting with V6O13 [31]. The above phenomena illustrated that the developed dual-signal sensors have good selectivity and anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of SPM and SPD in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Table S2 shows the results of SPM and SPD determination using mushrooms, pork, corn and passion fruit as actual samples. The results expressed that the sensor has achieved outstanding recovery rates of SPM and SPD on the colorimetric channel, with a recovery rate of 86.75%-98.47% and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.47%-8.47%. On the fluorescent channel, the average detection recoveries of SPM and SPD ranged from 85.30% to 98.98%, and the RSDs were 2.04–7.45%. The sensor was basically in agreement with HPLC. The above data indicated that the sensor had good practical applicability and accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reusability of dual-signal sensors
ce:section.ce:para: As revealed in Fig. S14A -B, the CDs and V6O13 nanobelts had outstanding storage time stability. Fig. S14C -D showed that the dual-signal sensors had great long-term stability and moisture resistance. The tremendous reusability of the dual-signal sensors was displayed in Fig. S14E -F. The results in Supporting Information demonstrated the dual-signal sensors had excellent stability and reusability.

In summary, the method employed the color change generated by V6O13 catalyzing TOPS and 4-AAP as a colorimetric signal, and the CDs fluorescence quenched by the PC1 was applied as fluorescent signals. A dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescent platform was constructed to simultaneously determine SPD and SPM. In the absence of SPM and SPD, the system exhibited higher PC1 UV absorption and lower CDs fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of PC1 on CDs was validated as SQE through fluorescence spectroscopy, fluorescence lifetime, UV absorption spectroscopy, and Stern Volmer linear equation. The peroxidase-like activity of V6O13 was inhibited in the presence of SPM and SPD, resulting in less PC1 production and more fluorescence restoration. Electrochemistry and free radical tracking tests indicated that SPM and SPD can suppress peroxidase activity by preventing electron transport between V6O13 and H2O2. As a result, an increased CDs fluorescence and a diminished PC1 UV absorption were associated with the contents of SPM and SPD, respectively, and they could be used for the quantitative detection of SPM and SPD. The established dual-signal sensors had good sensitivity and selectivity for detecting SPM and SPD. Compared with a single signal sensor, the results of colorimetric and fluorescent signals could be mutually validated, ensuring the accuracy of the system. Surprisingly, the sensor had been effectively employed for the SPM and SPD detection in food with good recoveries. The proposed strategy is expected to open up a novel path for constructing simple, affordable, and effective methods for simultaneously detecting SPM and SPD.

Yue Tang: Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis. Yuangen Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jiali Chen: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Siyu Wu: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Andi Huang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Lian Xia: Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32360621, 32160603, 31760486), the Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program (ZK[2021]Key Project 037), the Guizhou Provincial Key Technology R&D Program (QKHZC[2023]123), the Guizhou Provincial Outstanding Young Science and Technology Talent Project ([2021]5628), and the General Project for Technological Innovation and Development in the Field of Social Development in Guiyang City ([2023]13-18).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135596.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasound-assisted dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction combined with high performance liquid chromatography for sensitive determination of five biogenic amines in fermented fish samples

Srijaranai, Ultrasound-assisted dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction combined with high performance liquid chromatography for sensitive determination of five biogenic amines in fermented fish samples, Analytical Methods, 6(2014) 1128-1134.

Sens. Actuators B-Chem.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Andi Huang is currently a master student at School of Liquor and Food Engineering, Guizhou University. Her main research field is the detection of biogenic amine in foods based on nanozymes.
Lian Xia is currently a doctoral student at College of Life Sciences, Guizhou University. Her main research field is detection of food safety based on nanomaterials.
Jiali Chen is currently a college student at School of Liquor and Food Engineering, Guizhou University. Her specialty is Food Quality and Safety.
Siyu Wu is currently a college student at School of Liquor and Food Engineering, Guizhou University. Her specialty is Food Quality and Safety.
Yue Tang is currently a doctoral student at College of Life Sciences, Guizhou University. Her main research field is detection of food safety based on nanomaterials.

Yuangen Wu obtained his PhD degree in Environmental Engineering from Shanghai Jiao Tong University in 2014. He is currently a distinguished professor at School of Liquor and Food Engineering, Guizhou University. His research interests focus on bioanalysis and food safety detection based on functional nucleic acids and nanomaterials with mimetic enzyme.
